title: Literal Insert and Digraphs
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-V} for literals, {key:Ctrl-K} for digraphs — type the un-typeable.
summary: {key:Ctrl-V} (or {key:Ctrl-Q} on Windows) inserts the next keystroke literally — including special keys and Unicode codepoints. {key:Ctrl-K} combines two characters into one via the digraph table.

blocks.text: Sometimes you need to type a character that isn't on your keyboard, or you need to type a literal {key:Tab} that won't be expanded by autoindent. {key:Ctrl-V} and {key:Ctrl-K} are the escape hatches.

blocks.title: {key:Ctrl-V} vs {key:Ctrl-Q} on Windows
blocks.text: On Windows GUI Vim, {key:Ctrl-V} is mapped to *paste* (to match Windows convention). The literal-insert key there is {key:Ctrl-Q} — same function, alternate binding. On any system, {key:Ctrl-Q} works as a fallback (unless your terminal is consuming it for flow control, in which case `stty -ixon` frees it).

blocks.title: The Digraph Table
blocks.text:
Vim ships with a built-in table of "digraphs" — two-character mnemonics for harder-to-type Unicode characters. Run `:digraphs` to see all ~1500 entries. Most follow RFC 1345 conventions (`e'` = é, `OK` = ✓). You can add your own with `:digraph mn 9837` (define `mn` as ♭ codepoint 9837).

Using digraphs is faster than {key:Ctrl-V}{key:u} for characters you reach for often.
